text:
The purpose of this article is to present lexicographic and structural bases of the online dictionary of neologisms realised by the Language Observatory at the University of Warsaw. The dictionary is intended for the general public and at the same time for language research. It is based on the crowdsourcing model and uses a dictionary system in which new terms are entered by users and edited by professional linguists. The article presents in detail the micro-structure of the dictionary entries, including the traditional lexicographic part, as well as the categorical tags and notes added to the entries in order to enable efficient filtering of the data for research purposes.

While we will concentrate on the macro-structure of the lexicographical description of contemporary Polish in Contact, at this stage it is important to notice that the whole approach described is different from the traditional, prescriptive model of lexicography. By discussing language *in statu nascendi* as it occurs in digital communication and focusing on the unique features of newly-emerging words and expressions, the Observatory does not merely record, on an everyday basis, the institutional standards of Polish language units that were to come into use. It rather records, besides the latter, dynamic structures of present-day Polish in digital environment at various stages of their development and records also the unconventional phenomena that are not yet considered normative or, in fact, have not yet turned into stable terms — all these in the phase preceding their codification and becoming a part of language standards. The next theme will be the criteria for selection of primary language material used for lexicographical description of contemporary Polish in Contact, which we shall base on 2003 as the year of division; and then some basic theoretical issues will be raised which are crucial to determining the characteristics of new phenomena, and which can elucidate the problem of newness and define in which sense language units occurring in present-day digital communication and incorporated into the dictionary can be called *new*.
explanation: Reads as coherent, domain-specific academic prose with plausible terminology; there are no obvious AI signatures. However, highly polished AI could produce this, so the assessment is uncertain.
